description: The figure image is unreadable or not provided (displaying only a 'Check for updates' placeholder icon). Based on the accompanying text extraction summary, the figure likely presents comprehensive mechanical property data and microstructural correlations for the Fe-20Mn-6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel investigated at room temperature (RT), -77°C, and -196°C. The figure presumably includes engineering stress-strain curves demonstrating that yield strength increases significantly from 400 MPa at RT to 650 MPa at -77°C and 900 MPa at -196°C, while tensile elongation simultaneously improves from 32% to 58% and 68% respectively. Additional components may display Charpy impact energy absorption values showing a decreasing trend from 327 J at RT to 277 J at -77°C and 188 J at -196°C. The figure would also correlate these mechanical properties with deformation mechanism transitions from planar slip at RT to microband formation at -77°C and mechanical twinning at -196°C, as evidenced by EBSD and TEM characterization showing low-angle grain boundary fractions increasing from 56.0% to 70.2%. This supports the core scientific conclusion that reduced stacking fault energy at cryogenic temperatures promotes twinning-induced plasticity, enhancing strength-ductility synergy under quasi-static tension, but limits planar glide activation during high-strain-rate impact loading, resulting in reduced cryogenic toughness.
